title: ☐ VAT for Freelancers: Complete Guide Without Hassles
description:
Everything you need to know about VAT as a freelancer in the US/UK. Types of VAT, quarterly tax return, deductible VAT, equivalence surcharge regime, intra-community operations, and common errors with the tax authority.

VAT freelancers, quarterly tax return, deductible VAT, UK/US taxation, tax authority.

segments.voice: VAT is the tax that causes the most headaches for freelancers in the US/UK. Not because it's complicated, but because nobody explains it well. Errors in invoices, deductions you don't apply, returns you submit late. All that costs you money and problems with the tax authority. Today, I'm going to explain VAT for freelancers from start to finish, so you'll never have a shock with the tax authority again.
segments.visual: tax form frustration papers calculator desk

segments.voice: Let's start with the basics. VAT is an indirect tax that taxes consumption. As a freelancer, you don't pay VAT out of your pocket. You charge it to your clients and hand it over to the tax authority. You're an intermediary collector. The difference between the VAT you charge, the VAT you pass on, and the VAT you pay on your professional purchases, the VAT you bear, is what you pay to or get back from the tax authority each quarter.
segments.visual: vat flow diagram business collection payment cycle

segments.voice: There are three types of VAT in the US/UK. The standard: twenty-one percent, which applies to most products and professional services. The reduced: ten percent, for non-essential food, passenger transport, hospitality, home renovations, and cultural events. And the zero-rated: zero percent, for basic food, books, medications, and social housing.
segments.visual: three vat rates comparison chart percentages us/uk

segments.voice: What type of VAT do you apply on your invoices? It depends on your activity. If you're a programmer, designer, consultant, lawyer, or any professional service provider, you apply the twenty-one percent. If you have a restaurant, you apply the ten percent to food and drink. If you sell books, the zero percent. And be careful, there are activities exempt from VAT like regulated education, medical services, and insurance operations. According to tax law.
segments.visual: different business types services products vat rates

segments.voice: The quarterly tax return is the quarterly VAT declaration that every freelancer must submit. It's submitted in the first twenty days of the month following the quarter. That is: first quarter in April, second in July, third in October, and fourth in January of the following year. If you direct debit the payment, the deadline is extended by five days. If you're late, there's an automatic surcharge of one percent plus one additional percent for each month of delay.
segments.visual: calendar quarterly deadlines model 303 tax dates

segments.voice: Let's go with a practical example. In the first quarter, you issued invoices for a total of ten thousand dollars plus twenty-one percent VAT, which are two thousand one hundred dollars of VAT you passed on. At the same time, you had professional expenses of three thousand dollars plus VAT, which are six hundred thirty dollars of deductible VAT. The difference: two thousand one hundred minus six hundred thirty, equals one thousand four hundred seventy dollars. That's what you pay to the tax authority.
segments.visual: calculation example vat collected versus vat paid

segments.voice: What VAT can you deduct? The VAT of all purchases and expenses directly related to your professional activity. Office supplies, computer equipment, software, professional phone, work trips, training, professional insurance, and supplies for your premises. According to tax law. The key is that the expense is linked to your activity and that you have a complete invoice.
segments.visual: deductible expenses list receipts invoices organized

segments.voice: Essential requirements to deduct VAT. First: complete invoice with all the data. A receipt is not enough. The invoice must include the issuer's tax ID, your tax ID as the recipient, service or product description, taxable base, VAT rate, VAT amount, and date. Second: that the expense is recorded in your book of received invoices. Third: that the expense is linked to your economic activity.
segments.visual: invoice requirements checklist complete data fields

segments.voice: A very common mistake: deducting VAT from simplified invoices or receipts. A simplified invoice, what used to be a receipt, only allows VAT deduction if it includes your tax ID. If you buy from a stationery store and they give you a receipt without your tax ID, that VAT is not deductible. Solution: always ask for a complete invoice with your tax ID. It's your right as a professional according to tax law.
segments.visual: receipt versus full invoice comparison documents

segments.voice: VAT on cars is one of the most contentious issues. By default, you can only deduct fifty percent of the VAT on vehicle expenses: purchase, fuel, repairs, insurance. To deduct one hundred percent, you must prove that the vehicle is used exclusively for your activity, as in the case of taxi drivers, transporters, or sales representatives. According to tax law.
segments.visual: car business use fuel gas station receipt vehicle

segments.voice: Mixed expenses, those that are partially professional and partially personal, have special rules for VAT. The phone you use for work and personal use, the computer your children use to play, home supplies if you work from home. In these cases, you must apply a percentage of allocation to your activity. The tax authority accepts fifty percent as a reasonable criterion for mixed expenses, but may require justification.
segments.visual: mixed use expenses personal professional split ratio

segments.voice: Let's talk about the simplified VAT regime, also known as the flat rate scheme. If your activity is included in the list of activities for the flat rate scheme and you invoice less than a certain threshold, you can pay tax through a system of fixed quarterly quotas calculated based on factors like premises size, number of employees, or kilowatts of power. It's simpler administratively, but not always the most economically advantageous.
segments.visual: simplified regime modules calculation fixed quota

segments.voice: The equivalence surcharge. If you're a retail trader, an individual, and you sell products without transforming them, you're required to apply the equivalence surcharge regime. Your suppliers charge you the normal VAT plus an additional surcharge: five point two percent for the standard rate, one point four percent for the reduced rate, and zero point five percent for the zero-rated. In return, you don't submit the quarterly tax return or keep VAT records.
segments.visual: retail shop store equivalence surcharge merchant

segments.voice: Intra-community operations. If you sell services or products to companies in other EU countries, you don't charge UK/US VAT. The operation is subject to the reverse charge mechanism. But you need to register for a VAT number and submit the VAT return. If you don't, the tax authority can consider it a domestic transaction and claim the VAT.
segments.visual: european union trade international business map eu

segments.voice: To register for a VAT number, you need to apply through the tax authority's website. The tax authority usually carries out a check before granting you a VAT number. Once registered, always verify your clients' VAT numbers in the VIES system before issuing invoices without VAT.
segments.visual: vies system verification eu vat number check

segments.voice: Corrective invoices. If you made an error in an already issued invoice, don't erase or modify it. You must issue a corrective invoice that cancels or corrects the original. The corrective invoice must refer to the original invoice, indicate the correction made, and have its own numbering. According to tax law. This directly affects the VAT of the quarter in which you issue the correction.
segments.visual: corrective invoice amendment document correction

segments.voice: An error I see constantly: not declaring VAT on advances. If a client pays you an advance before you deliver the service, you must pass on the VAT at the time of receiving the advance, not when you deliver the service. According to tax law. Many freelancers wait until they issue the final invoice to declare all the VAT, and that's incorrect and can generate a parallel VAT settlement by the tax authority.
segments.visual: advance payment deposit invoice timing calendar

segments.voice: VAT refund. If in a quarter your deductible VAT exceeds the VAT you pass on, the result of your VAT return is negative. You have VAT in your favor. In the first three quarters, you can only offset this negative balance in the following quarters of the same year. The refund request can only be made in the fourth quarter, with the VAT return of the last period. The tax authority has six months to refund you.
segments.visual: vat refund negative balance compensation timeline

segments.voice: If the tax authority takes more than six months to refund your VAT, you're entitled to late payment interest. Many freelancers don't know this and don't claim it. If you submitted your refund request in January and by August you still haven't received the money, you have the right to claim the late payment interest. Submit a written request to the tax authority asking for the payment of the corresponding late payment interest. According to tax law.
segments.visual: refund delay interest claim document calendar wait

segments.voice: Making Tax Digital. If you invoice more than the VAT threshold, you're required to use Making Tax Digital, an electronic system for VAT records. But if you invoice less, which is the case for most freelancers, you can keep your VAT records in a traditional way. Even so, many invoicing programs already integrate Making Tax Digital and make it easier for you to submit your VAT return.
segments.visual: electronic invoicing system digital records computer

segments.voice: VAT records. Every freelancer must keep at least three records: the book of issued invoices, the book of received invoices, and the book of capital assets. If you carry out intra-community operations, you must also keep the book of certain intra-community operations. These records don't need an official format, but they must contain all the information of each invoice: date, number, tax ID, taxable base, VAT rate, and VAT amount.
segments.visual: accounting books records organized filing system

segments.voice: Errors that the tax authority detects automatically. The tax authority cross-checks the data from your VAT returns with those of your clients and suppliers. If your client declares that they paid you an invoice with VAT and you didn't declare it in your VAT return, the tax authority detects it. If you declare a deductible VAT from a supplier that doesn't match what the supplier declared as VAT they passed on, the tax authority also detects it. Discrepancies generate automatic requests.
segments.visual: computer cross checking data audit automatic alert

segments.voice: Summary of quarterly VAT obligations. Each quarter, you must submit the VAT return with the result of the VAT you passed on minus the VAT you bore. In January, in addition to the VAT return of the fourth quarter, you submit the annual VAT summary. If you have intra-community operations, you also submit the intra-community operations declaration. And if you make withholding taxes, the quarterly and annual withholding tax returns. Mark these dates on your calendar.
segments.visual: quarterly obligations checklist calendar deadlines

segments.voice: My final advice: automate VAT management. Use an invoicing program that automatically calculates VAT, records issued and received invoices, and generates a draft of your VAT return. The cost of good invoicing software is between ten and thirty dollars a month. Compare it to the minimum fine from the tax authority for submitting the VAT return late: one hundred dollars. Automation pays for itself.
segments.visual: accounting software dashboard automated invoicing

segments.voice: VAT shouldn't keep you up at night. It's a pass-through tax, not coming out of your pocket. Charge the correct VAT, deduct everything you can with an invoice, submit the VAT return on time, and keep your records up to date. With that, you'll never have problems with the tax authority. Subscribe and turn on the notification bell for more practical tax content for freelancers. Share this video with someone who's just starting out. See you in the next one.
segments.visual: subscribe button notification bell tax peace mind
